observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to file a request with the COA for the write-off of the dormant and/or unliquidated cash advances amounting to P404,467.50 following the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant commented that she will prepare the necessary documents for the filing of request for authority to write off the dormant unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: The Municipality still in the process of the preparation of documents for the filing of request with the COA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all PPE at least once a
year, prepare and submit a copy of the RPCPPE to the Auditor not later
than January 31 of each year; and coordinate closely with the Municipal
Accounting Office (MAO) to reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting
records;
b) MGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein relevant
information for proper monitoring; and
c) Municipal Accountant to maintain PPE Ledger Cards for individual
property items with complete details of the PPE.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to properly record the purchase and receipt of welfare goods for
distribution to various beneficiaries of the Municipality to Welfare Goods for
Distribution account and the corresponding issuance thereof to Welfare
Goods Expense account, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to facilitate the filing of request for authority to write off
the dormant and/or unliquidated cash advances amounting to P404,467.50 to
the Commission on Audit (COA) following the guidelines and procedures
provided by COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and other concerned Officials to submit to COA the lacking
documents to support the payment of the purchase of lot and to explain in
writing why the sale was consummated despite the pronouncement of the
MAC that the subject property was also owned by another entity to avoid
possible suspension/disallowance in audit. Likewise, work on the immediate
transfer of ownership of the property to the Municipality and submit a certified
true copy of the new Transfer Certificate of Title to the Audit Team.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to inquire from the Department of Budget
and Management (DBM) and/or Bureau of the Treasury on the proper
procedures on how subject dormant funds could be returned to the national
government given that pertinent records could not anymore be traced by the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to immediately submit the identified disbursement vouchers,
liquidation reports and their supporting documents to the COA to avoid
suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions in audit. Henceforth,
ensure that all accounts are submitted within the first ten days of the ensuing
month pursuant to Section 7.2 of the COA RRSA.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team for further evaluation
all Accomplishment Reports on the number of cubic meter of garbage
dumped in the landfill certified correct by the MENRO, Trip tickets for
Check Nos. 547649, 567115, 567237, 567773 and 568068 as proof of
services and Contractor’s Bill for Check No. 568068, to avoid audit
suspensions/disallowances;
b) Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to explain why the amount of
P188,813.30 as payment for CY 2019 was charged to current appropriation
and sanitation fee was paid even if it was not included in the Contract
Agreement for CY 2020, for evaluation of the Audit Team; and
c) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and other concerned officials to study
the legal possibility of amending the contract for the purpose of clarifying
the scope of work of the Contractor to enable the Audit Team and the COA
Technical Audit Specialists to conduct a well-informed auditorial review
and technical evaluation of the contract.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to investigate and account the financial
assistance intended for 78 beneficiaries amounting to P54,600.00 which were
considered and reported to have been liquidated despite absence of
signatures in the payrolls or other proof that they received the benefits,
otherwise refund the said amount.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team justifications on its inability
to comply with the posting requirement under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020,
for further evaluation; and
b) BAC Secretariat and the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit
Team the Mayor’s or Business Permit, including the Income/Business Tax
Returns and Omnibus Sworn Statement (OSS) for projects with an ABC of
P500,000.00, and the Philippine Contractors Accreditation Board (PCAB)
License and Net Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) of the
suppliers/contractors pertaining to COVID-19 expenditures totaling
P57,654,925.49 to avoid issuance of audit suspension.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly certified list showing the names of current taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the current year to enable the latter Office to set up the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year; and b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate the cause/s of the net difference of P4,300,215.09 between the RPT and SET balances of the Receivables and P27,934,205.30 between the said accounts and the aggregate amount of these two accounts and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31, 2019, and make the necessary adjustments in their records.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer already furnished the Municipal Accounting Office the correct amount to be set up in the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Economic Enterprises Development and Management Officer/Market Supervisor to: a) review existing Contracts of Lease and/or facilitate the execution of formal agreements with stallholders who have no contracts yet with the Municipality, making sure that the contracts are prepared in accordance with relevant Municipal Ordinances for market operations and its amendments; b) send demand letters to delinquent stallholders to collect the market rental fees, goodwill and arkabala totaling P4,057,035.58 plus the corresponding interests and penalties, with the reminder that their failure to settle their accounts within 60 days from due date shall be a sufficient cause for the revocation of the lease and for the ejection of the stallholders from his/her stall/booth; c) require all market stallholders to secure Mayor’s or business permits and licenses, otherwise, impose upon them the sanctions or penalties provided under relevant Municipal ordinance/s; d) investigate and explain the records discrepancies between the MEEDMO and BPLO records, specifically on the 50 stallholders who secured business permits from the BPLO but whose names did not appear in the record of the MEEDMO as stallholders; e) update and complete the records of market stallholders to include relevant information such as their names, residential addresses, contact numbers, business permits and license numbers and issuance dates, goodwill, market rental fees and/or arkabala, electricity and water bills due from them, amount paid, OR numbers and date of issuances, and outstanding balance/s, if any, and other data that would facilitate the proper monitoring of their compliance with the Municipal Ordinances and its amendments; f) submit the list or records of delinquent stallholders to the Municipal Accounting Office as of December 31, 2019 for proper recording in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and g) henceforth, ensure that the provisions of the Municipal Ordinances and its amendments regarding market operations, particularly those related to the collection of market fees, imposition of interests and penalties for late or non-payment of fees, revocation of the lease of stalls and securing of business permit and license are strictly enforced.
observations.managementAction: Reviewed the existing Market Code and facilitate the issuance of contract to those non holders. Reviewed the non implemented projects and were implemented reprogramed to covid related expenses.
observations.reason: Due to pandemic the Municipality was not able to collect all delinquent market stallholders.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting further cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash advances and institute measures to ensure that cash advances are liquidated on time. It is also recommended that the Municipal Accountant be instructed to send demand letters to officials and employees emphasizing that their failure to settle the cash advances despite receipt of the demand letters shall constitute a valid cause for withholding of their salaries.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant sent demand letters to those employees who have an unliquidated cash advances emphasizing that their failure to settle the cash advances despite receipt of the demand letters shall constitute a valid cause for withholding of their salaries.
observations.reason: Cash Advances were still granted to personnel with outstanding cash advances during the pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) remit immediately the amount of P350,106.17 due and payable to the BIR and to personally shoulder the surcharges and interests which may arise from late remittances; and b) henceforth, remit fully the withheld taxes to the BIR within 10 days following the end of the month the withholding was made in compliance with Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.
observations.managementAction: Remitted the balance to the BIR and the Accounting Office assured compliance with the law regarding the withholding of taxes. Remittance of withheld taxes were made within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to incorporate in the 20 per cent DF Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the detailed schedules/timelines in the implementation of projects; and b) Municipal Development Council and MPDC to review the non implemented projects and determine whether these are still feasible and should be priority projects of the Municipality. Otherwise, re-program the funds allotted for such projects including any unexpended balance of completed projects with the concurrence of the Sangguniang Bayan, for other development projects that will address the present needs of the Municipality and its constituents, and ensure that corresponding timelines for the reprogrammed/new projects are set.
observations.managementAction: Timelines in the implementation of projects where incorporated in the AIP. Reviewed the non implemented projects and were implemented reprogramed to covid related expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to provide accurate timetables or schedules of the implementation of preparedness related projects and activities in the LDRRMFIP for proper guidance of all concerned personnel; and to closely monitor the LDRRMF utilization to realize the objectives and goals of the Municipality as embodied in the LDRRM Plan for 2020-2022.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO had closely monitored its annual LDRRMF utilization to realize the objectives and goals of the Municipality as embodied in the LDRRM Plan for 2020-2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to inquire from the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and/or Bureau of the Treasury on the proper procedures on how subject dormant funds could be returned to the national government given that pertinent records could not anymore be traced by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Initially inquired thru DBM about the concerned matter.
observations.reason: Unexpended balances were not yet returned to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWDO to immediately inform the DSWD Regional Office No. IV-A of the issue regarding the absence of focal person to supervise the training programs and request for his/her replacement.
observations.managementAction: The MSWD has already initiated communication with the Regional Office regarding the absence of their staff. Continuous coordination regarding the replacement shall be followed-up within the month. The project will be implemented within the 1st quarter of 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that concerned officials to prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property of all the unserviceable property with complete information to facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the same, after which ensure their immediate disposal to prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.
observations.managementAction: The unserviceable property were already disposed of through sale.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with all the concerned officials, review and re-establish the composition of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) of the Municipality to conform with the requirements of PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: Membership has already been amended through Executive Order No. 11 series of 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the
Municipal Accounting Office with the duly
certified list showing the names of delinquent
real property taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year 2019 to enable the latter
Office to set up the RPT and SET Receivables
for the current year correctly; and

b. direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate
the cause/s of the net difference of
P490,875.37 between the balances of the RPT
and SET Receivables and P24,116,411.74 between
the aggregate amount of these two accounts and
the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31,
2018, and make the necessary adjustments in
their records.

Reiterated in audit observation No. 1.
observations.reason:
As of December 31, 2019, unreconciled net
differences of P4,300,215.09 between the balances
of the RPT and SET Receivables and P27,934,205.30
between the Accounting and Treasury Offices’
records.
Reiterated in audit observation No. 1

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. adhere to the provision of Section 5 of DILG-DBM
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 to ensure the
optimal utilization of the 20 per cent DF to help
achieve the desirable socio-economic development
and environmental outcomes of the LGU; and

b. mobilize the concerned offices in early planning and
implementation of development projects to avoid
incurring possible additional cost as a result of delayed
implementation.

Discussed in audit observation No. 6
observations.managementAction:
The MPDC stated that they would activate the
Project Implementation Team to identify timelines of
each PPA’s included in the 20 per cent Development
Fund. They also plan to meet with the Municipal
Development Council to determine which PPAs
should be prioritized and the feasibility of their
implementation.
observations.reason:
Twelve development projects under the 20 per cent
Development Fund (DF) of the Municipality aggregating
P18,000,000.00 or 31.27 per cent of the total Calendar
Year (CY) 2019 appropriation of P57,557,082.00 were
not implemented.
Reiterated in audit observation No. 6

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly
certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year 2020 to enable the latter Office to set up the RPT and
SET Receivables for the current year correctly; and
b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate
the cause/s of the net difference of P4,300,215.09 between the balances of the
RPT and SET Receivables and P27,934,205.30 between the aggregate amount
of these two accounts and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31, 2019,
and make the necessary adjustments in their records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal
Economic Enterprises Development and Management Officer/Market Supervisor
to:
a) review existing Contracts of Lease and/or facilitate the execution of formal
agreements with market stallholders who have no contracts yet with the
Municipality, making sure that the contracts are prepared in accordance with
the relevant Municipal Ordinances for market operations and its
amendments;
b) send demand letters to delinquent stallholders to collect the market stall
rental fees, goodwill and arkabala totaling P4,057,035.58 plus the
corresponding interests and penalties, with the reminder that their failure to
settle their accounts within 60 days from due date shall be a sufficient cause
for the revocation of the lease and for the ejection of the stallholders from
his/her stall/booth;
c) require all market stallholders to secure Mayor’s or business permits and
licenses, otherwise, impose upon them the sanctions or penalties provided
under relevant Municipal ordinance/s;
d) investigate and explain the discrepancies between the MEEDMO and BPLO
records, specifically on the 50 stallholders who secured business permits
from the BPLO but whose names did not appear in the record of the MEEDMO
as stallholders;
e) update and complete the records of market stallholders to include relevant
information such as their names, residential addresses, contact numbers,
business permits and license numbers and issuance dates, goodwill, market
rental fees and/or arkabala, electricity and water bills due from them, amount
paid, OR numbers and date of issuances, and outstanding balance/s, if any,
and other data that would facilitate the proper monitoring of their compliance
with the Municipal Ordinances and its amendments;
f) submit the list or records of delinquent stallholders accounts to the Municipal
Accounting Office as of December 31, 2019 for proper recording in the books
of accounts of the Municipality; and
g) henceforth, ensure that the provisions of the Municipal Ordinances and its
amendments regarding market operations, particularly those related to the
collection of market fees, imposition of interests and penalties for late or non-
payment of fees, revocation of the lease of stalls and securing of business
permit and license are strictly enforced.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to facilitate the reconciliation of the discrepancies of P1,016,408.72
between the Accounting and MEEDMO records of receivables from market
operations as of December 31, 2019, and henceforth, ensure that all collections and
receivables from the market operations are completely and correctly accounted in the
books of accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
file a request with the COA for the write-off of the dormant and/or unliquidated
cash advances amounting to P404,467.50 following the procedures provided
under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
refrain from granting further cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash
advances and to institute measures to ensure that cash advances are liquidated
on time. It is also recommended that the Municipal Accountant be instructed to
send demand letters to officials and employees with long outstanding cash
advances emphasizing that failure to settle the cash advances despite receipt of
the demand letters shall constitute a valid cause for withholding of their salaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) remit immediately the amount of P350,106.17 due to BIR and to direct
concerned official/s to personally shoulder the surcharges and interests
which may arise from late remittances; and
b) henceforth, remit the withheld taxes fully to the BIR within 10 days following
the end of the month the withholding was made in compliance with Revenue
Regulation No. 2-98.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to incorporate in
the 20 per cent DF Annual Investment Plan the detailed schedules/timelines
in the implementation of the programs/projects/ activities for the proper
guidance of all concerned personnel; and
b) Municipal Development Council and MPDC to review the non implemented
projects and determine whether these are still feasible and should be priority
projects of the Municipality. Otherwise, re-program the funds allotted for
such projects including any unexpended balance of completed projects with
the concurrence of the Sangguniang Bayan, for other development projects
that will address the present needs of the Municipality and its constituents,
and ensure that corresponding timelines for the reprogrammed/ new projects
are set.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO and the
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to provide accurate
information on the timetables or schedules of the implementation of disaster
prevention and mitigation, and preparedness related projects and activities in the
LDRRMFIP for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Treasurer to inquire from the Department of Budget and Management (DBM)
and/or Bureau of the Treasury on the proper procedures on how subject dormant
funds could be returned to the national government given that pertinent records
could not anymore be traced by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWDO to immediately
inform the DSWD Regional Office No. IV-A of the issue regarding the absence of
focal person to supervise the training programs and request for his/her
replacement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned Property officials to
prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee an Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property of all the unserviceable property
with complete information to facilitate the determination of the appraised value
and mode of disposal of the same, after which ensure their immediate disposal to
prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with all the concerned
officials, review and re-establish the composition of the GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) of the Municipality to conform with the requirements of PCW-DBM-DILG-
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that
the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to
conduct a comprehensive and
complete physical count of all the
property and the control
corresponding Physical Count of
number for
Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) be
submitted to the
Office of the Auditor
on or before
January 31 of each
year;
b. General Services
Officer (GSO) to
prepare and submit to
the Municipal
Accountant the
necessary documents
of the disposed assets
for dropping the
amount of P1,463,000.00
representing the cost of
disposed assets in the
books of accounts;
c. Municipal Accountant
and the General
Services Officer to
analyze and reconcile
the discrepancies in
the balances of the
PPE accounts between
the accounting records
and inventory reports
and make the
necessary adjustments
and regularly reconcile
the result of the
physical count with the
property and
accounting records; and
d. Municipal Accountant
and GSO to prepare/
maintain PPELCs, RPLCs
and Property Cards,
and ensure its
completeness as to the
necessary information
for proper recording,
monitoring and control
of the assets of the
Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee to continue to
conducting a complete physical count of all the
property and equipment of the Municipality.
They are putting asset tags/stickers with control
number for recording and determination/confirm
its actual existence, and the corresponding
accounts.
The General Services Officer to prepare and
submit to the Municipal Accountant the
necessary documents of the disposed assets and
were dropped from the books of accounts.
The discrepancies in the balances between the
accounting records and inventory reports had
decreased from P104,262,261.81 to only
P2,133,014.20 as of December 31, 2019.
The GSO already maintains Property Cards.
PPELCs and RPLCs were not yet maintained by
the Accounting Office.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee is still conducting a
complete physical count of all the property. The
Inventory Committee continue to conducting a
complete physical count of all the property. They
are putting asset tags/stickers with control number
for recording and

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior
recommendations that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. The concerned Office/ officers/employees to
immediately liquidate their outstanding cash
advances immediately especially at year-end and
see to it that all cash advances granted to them are
liquidated within the prescribed period;

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold the
salaries of the accountable officers who failed to
liquidate their long outstanding cash advances; 

c. Refrain from granting additional cash advances to
any official or employee unless the previous cash
advance given to him is first settled or a proper
accounting thereof is made; and

d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to trace back the
validity of the recorded amount of P404,467.50 to
the Advances to Officers and Employees account in
the General Fund and if pertinent documents
cannot be located or collections are remote, take
appropriate action to reflect the corrected balance
of the account.
observations.managementAction:
a) The Accounting Office has already sent demand
letters to those concerned officers and employees to
liquidate their outstanding cash advances.

b) The Municipal Accountant already required
concerned officials and employees to be guided by
the prescribed regulations and see to it that all
cash advances are liquidated within the prescribed
period.

c) The Municipal Accountant had traced back the
books of accounts from Calendar Year (CY) 1998.
However, despite diligent efforts, documents to
support the cash advances could no longer be
found.

There was still unliquidated cash advances amounting
to P1,059,252.50 as of December 31, 2019.
observations.reason:
As of December 31, 2019 outstanding cash advances
amounted to P1,059,252.50.
There were still cash advances that were liquidated
beyond the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Accountant observe the proper accounts
to be used for the receipt of funds to safeguard
against inaccurate or misleading information.
Moreover, make the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant has already reclassify the
accounts and observed proper accounts.
Moreover, make the necessary adjusting entries.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Accountant evaluate the above-mentioned
expenditures amounting to P602,877.33 and
P188,720.00 and effect the necessary adjusting
entries and to reclassify the accounts. Thereafter,
draw the necessary Journal Entry Voucher/s to record
and effect the necessary adjustments for fairer
presentation of the accounts of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant
locate and submit to the Office of the Auditor the
Deed of Donation for the expenditures totaling
P199,340.00 for further evaluation of the subject
disbursements.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary
adjusting entries amounting to P602,877.33 and
P188,720.00 recorded the accounts. 
The Municipal Accountant was to locate and submit to
the Office of the Auditor the Deed of Donation for the
expenditures totaling P199,340.00 for further
evaluation of the subject disbursements.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team was still waiting for the submission of
Deed of Donations.

observations.recommendation:
We also recommended that henceforth, the Municipal
Mayor ensure strict adherence to the provisions of the
afore-cited JMC on the use of the 20 per cent
Development Fund to attain the objectives for which
the fund was established and avoid further suspensions
or disallowances in audit.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality complied with the provisions of DILG-
DBM Joint Memorandum Circular JMC No. 2017-1 and
stopped charging the 20 per cent Development Fund for
non-priority expenditures. The Municipality complied
with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum
Circular JMC No. 2017-1

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Human Resource and
Management Officer (HRMO) inform the persons liable
to immediately settle the subject disallowances.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal
Accountant record as receivables the amount covered
by NFD received as “Receivables – Disallowances/
Charges”.
observations.managementAction:
The HRMO already informed the persons liable to
settle their disallowances. Some paid their disallowances
through monthly salary deduction.
The Municipal Accountant recorded the amount of
P40,032.00 in the books of accounts received as
“Receivables – Disallowances/Charges”.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct the concerned offices or departments to
draw up appropriate project proposals to utilize fully
idle or dormant trust funds pursuant to the law
governing said grant and/or the Memorandum of
Agreement with the respective donors;

b. direct the proper liquidation of the amount granted as
well as the refund of excess project funds to the donor,
if so provided in the terms of the grant; and/or

c. to seek approval from the trustor to revert the
balances of these long outstanding dormant accounts,
which purposes were no longer known, to the LGU’s
unappropriated surplus of the General Fund so that
these will become a source of fund of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Office exerted their full effort to locate
and the documents or records regarding the dormant
account. The Municipal Mayor had instructed the
Municipal Accountant to comply with the audit
recommendation.
observations.reason:
Absence of records or documents to establish the
account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Municipal Accountant and the concerned employees
to be guided by the prescribed regulations and see to it
that all travel claims conform thereto prior to
processing them for payment.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant required the concerned
employees to be guided by the prescribed regulations
and see to it that all travel claims conform thereto
prior to processing them for payment. Computation
were done in accordance with EO No. 77, series of
2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the BAC members provide the
Office of the Auditor written justification for the
conduct of bidding process ahead of the earliest possible
time for the evaluation and for the incurrence of
recommended delay in the procurement of one contract
costing P1,050,000.00 for evaluation.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC added members of the BAC Secretariat to see to
it that all documents are in accordance with RA 9184.
observations.reason: There were still delay in the procurement process.
